Published during the Edo period, it was soon identified as a {{ill|forgery|en|forgery|WD=Q1332286|lt=forgery}} by researchers and its associated parties were punished by the shogunate. It is also referred to as simply the "Taiseikyō" (大成経).
The {{ill|forgery|en|forgery|WD=Q1332286|lt=forgery}} was based on the already disputed {{ill|Kujiki|en|Kujiki|WD=Q2716751|lt=Sendai Kuji Hongi}}, a text considered, from the mid-Heian period to the mid-Edo period, one of the essential classical historical texts alongside the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}. During the Edo period, the Sendai Kuji Hongi (composed of ten volumes) was published with the claim that it was merely an excerpt, and the original was this "Sendai Kuji Hongi Taiseikyō."
== Overview == According to the preface of this text, it was commissioned in 620 by Empress Suiko, aiming to clarify historical achievements for the revival of Shinto and national prosperity. Prince Shōtoku and {{ill|Soga no Umako|en|Soga no Umako|WD=Q1191233|lt=Soga no Umako}} are said to have collected records from the imperial court and various prominent families. When unclear records of the Age of the Gods were encountered, documents were allegedly discovered in a sacred repository belonging to the ancestral deities of the {{ill|Imibe|ja|忌部|1=Inbe clan|lt=Inbe|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imibe|13=User:Immanuelle/Inbe clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 忌部氏}} and {{ill|Radical 25|en|Radical 25|WD=Q94724|lt=Urabe}} clans, upon which Prince Shōtoku based his compilation.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://core.ac.uk/download/pdf/15917108.pdf |title=The "Shindai Kōtai Taiseikyō" Preface |access-date=2024-09-13 |publisher=Tokyo Gakugei University}}</ref>
The text appeared in the late Edo period, during a time when rationality was emphasized following the Confucian-Buddhist debates. It adopts a syncretic perspective, integrating Shinto, Confucianism, and Buddhism.
Despite being declared a forgery by the shogunate, it had influential advocates, including prominent intellectuals and officials of the time. For example, Nagano Uji used it as a reference for {{ill|Mononobe Shinto|ja|物部神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe Shinto|1=Mononobe Shinto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895461}}, and figures like daimyo {{ill|Kuroda Naoaki|ja|黒田直邦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Naoaki|1=Kuroda Naokuni|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678653}}, scholar {{ill|Henmui|ja|偏無為|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Henmui|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and monk {{ill|Tōrei Enji|en|Tōrei Enji|WD=Q17426718|lt=Tōrei Enji}} were deeply involved with the text.<ref name=":2" />
== Contents == Many versions of the text exist. The most well-known is the 72-volume edition published in Edo in 1679, with 40 volumes released at the time, including two appendices.
The work is divided into 38 volumes of main content and 34 volumes of auxiliary content, encompassing a wide range of topics, including history, rituals, divination, medical science, prophecy, and constitutional principles, asserting itself as a canonical text of Shinto.
=== Main Content === * '''Chronicle of the Age of the Gods''' (神代本紀): 1 volume. * '''Chronicle of Pre-Heaven''' (先天本紀): 1 volume. * '''Chronicle of Yin and Yang''' (陰陽本紀): 1 volume. * '''Chronicle of Yomi''' (黄泉本紀): 1 volume. * '''Chronicle of Kami''' (神祇本紀): 2 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Rituals''' (神事本紀): 2 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Heavenly Deities''' (天神本紀): 2 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Earthly Deities''' (地祇本紀): 2 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Imperial Descendants''' (皇孫本紀): 2 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Heavenly Descendants''' (天孫本紀): 2 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Divine Sovereigns''' (神皇本紀): 6 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Heavenly Sovereigns''' (天皇本紀): 6 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Imperial Sovereigns''' (帝皇本紀): 6 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Sacred Sovereigns''' (聖皇本紀): 4 volumes.
=== Auxiliary Content === * '''Chronicle of Teachings''' (經教本紀): 6 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Prayers''' (祝言本紀): 1 volume. * '''Chronicle of Heavenly Governance''' (天政本紀): 1 volume. * '''Chronicle of Divination''' (太占本紀): 2 volumes. * '''Chronicle of History''' (歴道本紀): 4 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Medical Practices''' (醫綱本紀): 4 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Rites''' (禮綱本紀): 4 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Songs''' (詠歌本紀): 2 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Teachings''' (御語本紀): 4 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Military Affairs''' (軍旅本紀): 2 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Predictions''' (未然本紀): 1 volume. * '''Chronicle of Constitutional Principles''' (憲法本紀): 1 volume. * '''Chronicle of Shrines''' (神社本紀): 1 volume. * '''Chronicle of Provincial Governors''' (國造本紀): 1 volume.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kujiki|en|Kujiki|WD=Q2716751|lt=Sendai Kuji Hongi}} * {{ill|Mononobe Shinto|ja|物部神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe Shinto|1=Mononobe Shinto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895461}}
== References == {{reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/sendaikuji/taisei.htm Sendai Kuji Hongi Taiseikyō] * {{Wayback|url=http://www.ceres.dti.ne.jp/~alex-x/shosai/gisi-001.html |title=World of Forged Histories and Texts |date=1999-08-27}} * [http://www.mars.dti.ne.jp/~techno/column/sendai.htm Sendai Kuji Hongi and Taiseikyō]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

The work is divided into 38 volumes of main content and 34 volumes of auxiliary content, encompassing a wide range of topics, including history, rituals, divination, medical science, prophecy, and constitutional principles, asserting itself as a canonical text of Shinto.
=== Main Content === * '''Chronicle of the Age of the Gods''' (神代本紀): 1 volume. * '''Chronicle of Pre-Heaven''' (先天本紀): 1 volume. * '''Chronicle of Yin and Yang''' (陰陽本紀): 1 volume. * '''Chronicle of Yomi''' (黄泉本紀): 1 volume. * '''Chronicle of Kami''' (神祇本紀): 2 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Rituals''' (神事本紀): 2 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Heavenly Deities''' (天神本紀): 2 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Earthly Deities''' (地祇本紀): 2 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Imperial Descendants''' (皇孫本紀): 2 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Heavenly Descendants''' (天孫本紀): 2 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Divine Sovereigns''' (神皇本紀): 6 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Heavenly Sovereigns''' (天皇本紀): 6 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Imperial Sovereigns''' (帝皇本紀): 6 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Sacred Sovereigns''' (聖皇本紀): 4 volumes.
=== Auxiliary Content === * '''Chronicle of Teachings''' (經教本紀): 6 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Prayers''' (祝言本紀): 1 volume. * '''Chronicle of Heavenly Governance''' (天政本紀): 1 volume. * '''Chronicle of Divination''' (太占本紀): 2 volumes. * '''Chronicle of History''' (歴道本紀): 4 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Medical Practices''' (醫綱本紀): 4 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Rites''' (禮綱本紀): 4 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Songs''' (詠歌本紀): 2 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Teachings''' (御語本紀): 4 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Military Affairs''' (軍旅本紀): 2 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Predictions''' (未然本紀): 1 volume. * '''Chronicle of Constitutional Principles''' (憲法本紀): 1 volume. * '''Chronicle of Shrines''' (神社本紀): 1 volume. * '''Chronicle of Provincial Governors''' (國造本紀): 1 volume.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kujiki|en|Kujiki|WD=Q2716751|lt=Sendai Kuji Hongi}} * {{ill|Mononobe Shinto|ja|物部神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe Shinto|1=Mononobe Shinto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895461}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:先代旧事本紀大成経]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:先代旧事本紀大成経]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:先代旧事本紀大成経]]
'''Sendai Kuji Hongi Taiseikyō''' (先代旧事本紀大成経, Sendai Kuji Hongi Taiseikyō) is a [[:en:scripture|scripture]] traditionally said to have been compiled under the command of {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko}} by {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku}}. Published during the Edo period, it was soon identified as a {{ill|forgery|en|forgery|WD=Q1332286|lt=forgery}} by researchers and its associated parties were punished by the shogunate. It is also referred to as simply the "Taiseikyō" (大成経).
The {{ill|forgery|en|forgery|WD=Q1332286|lt=forgery}} was based on the already disputed {{ill|Kujiki|en|Kujiki|WD=Q2716751|lt=Sendai Kuji Hongi}}, a text considered, from the mid-Heian period to the mid-Edo period, one of the essential classical historical texts alongside the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}. During the Edo period, the Sendai Kuji Hongi (composed of ten volumes) was published with the claim that it was merely an excerpt, and the original was this "Sendai Kuji Hongi Taiseikyō."
== Overview == According to the preface of this text, it was commissioned in 620 by Empress Suiko, aiming to clarify historical achievements for the revival of Shinto and national prosperity. Prince Shōtoku and {{ill|Soga no Umako|en|Soga no Umako|WD=Q1191233|lt=Soga no Umako}} are said to have collected records from the imperial court and various prominent families. When unclear records of the Age of the Gods were encountered, documents were allegedly discovered in a sacred repository belonging to the ancestral deities of the {{ill|Imibe|ja|忌部|1=Inbe clan|lt=Inbe|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imibe|13=User:Immanuelle/Inbe clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 忌部氏}} and {{ill|Radical 25|en|Radical 25|WD=Q94724|lt=Urabe}} clans, upon which Prince Shōtoku based his compilation.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://core.ac.uk/download/pdf/15917108.pdf |title=The "Shindai Kōtai Taiseikyō" Preface |access-date=2024-09-13 |publisher=Tokyo Gakugei University}}</ref>
The text appeared in the late Edo period, during a time when rationality was emphasized following the Confucian-Buddhist debates. It adopts a syncretic perspective, integrating Shinto, Confucianism, and Buddhism.
Despite being declared a forgery by the shogunate, it had influential advocates, including prominent intellectuals and officials of the time. For example, Nagano Uji used it as a reference for {{ill|Mononobe Shinto|ja|物部神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe Shinto|1=Mononobe Shinto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895461}}, and figures like daimyo {{ill|Kuroda Naoaki|ja|黒田直邦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Naoaki|1=Kuroda Naokuni|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678653}}, scholar {{ill|Henmui|ja|偏無為|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Henmui|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and monk {{ill|Tōrei Enji|en|Tōrei Enji|WD=Q17426718|lt=Tōrei Enji}} were deeply involved with the text.<ref name=":2" />
== Contents == Many versions of the text exist. The most well-known is the 72-volume edition published in Edo in 1679, with 40 volumes released at the time, including two appendices.
The work is divided into 38 volumes of main content and 34 volumes of auxiliary content, encompassing a wide range of topics, including history, rituals, divination, medical science, prophecy, and constitutional principles, asserting itself as a canonical text of Shinto.
=== Main Content === * '''Chronicle of the Age of the Gods''' (神代本紀): 1 volume. * '''Chronicle of Pre-Heaven''' (先天本紀): 1 volume. * '''Chronicle of Yin and Yang''' (陰陽本紀): 1 volume. * '''Chronicle of Yomi''' (黄泉本紀): 1 volume. * '''Chronicle of Kami''' (神祇本紀): 2 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Rituals''' (神事本紀): 2 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Heavenly Deities''' (天神本紀): 2 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Earthly Deities''' (地祇本紀): 2 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Imperial Descendants''' (皇孫本紀): 2 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Heavenly Descendants''' (天孫本紀): 2 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Divine Sovereigns''' (神皇本紀): 6 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Heavenly Sovereigns''' (天皇本紀): 6 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Imperial Sovereigns''' (帝皇本紀): 6 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Sacred Sovereigns''' (聖皇本紀): 4 volumes.
=== Auxiliary Content === * '''Chronicle of Teachings''' (經教本紀): 6 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Prayers''' (祝言本紀): 1 volume. * '''Chronicle of Heavenly Governance''' (天政本紀): 1 volume. * '''Chronicle of Divination''' (太占本紀): 2 volumes. * '''Chronicle of History''' (歴道本紀): 4 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Medical Practices''' (醫綱本紀): 4 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Rites''' (禮綱本紀): 4 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Songs''' (詠歌本紀): 2 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Teachings''' (御語本紀): 4 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Military Affairs''' (軍旅本紀): 2 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Predictions''' (未然本紀): 1 volume. * '''Chronicle of Constitutional Principles''' (憲法本紀): 1 volume. * '''Chronicle of Shrines''' (神社本紀): 1 volume. * '''Chronicle of Provincial Governors''' (國造本紀): 1 volume.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kujiki|en|Kujiki|WD=Q2716751|lt=Sendai Kuji Hongi}} * {{ill|Mononobe Shinto|ja|物部神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe Shinto|1=Mononobe Shinto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895461}}
== References == {{reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/sendaikuji/taisei.htm Sendai Kuji Hongi Taiseikyō] * {{Wayback|url=http://www.ceres.dti.ne.jp/~alex-x/shosai/gisi-001.html |title=World of Forged Histories and Texts |date=1999-08-27}} * [http://www.mars.dti.ne.jp/~techno/column/sendai.htm Sendai Kuji Hongi and Taiseikyō]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:先代旧事本紀大成経]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:先代旧事本紀大成経]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:先代旧事本紀大成経]]
'''Sendai Kuji Hongi Taiseikyō''' (先代旧事本紀大成経, Sendai Kuji Hongi Taiseikyō) is a [[:en:scripture|scripture]] traditionally said to have been compiled under the command of {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko}} by {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}}. Published during the Edo period, it was soon identified as a {{ill|forgery|en|forgery|WD=Q1332286|lt=forgery|1=Forgery}} by researchers and its associated parties were punished by the shogunate. It is also referred to as simply the "Taiseikyō" (大成経).
The {{ill|forgery|en|forgery|WD=Q1332286|lt=forgery|1=Forgery}} was based on the already disputed {{ill|Kujiki|en|Kujiki|WD=Q2716751|lt=Sendai Kuji Hongi|1=Kujiki}}, a text considered, from the mid-Heian period to the mid-Edo period, one of the essential classical historical texts alongside the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}. During the Edo period, the Sendai Kuji Hongi (composed of ten volumes) was published with the claim that it was merely an excerpt, and the original was this "Sendai Kuji Hongi Taiseikyō."
== Overview == According to the preface of this text, it was commissioned in 620 by Empress Suiko, aiming to clarify historical achievements for the revival of Shinto and national prosperity. Prince Shōtoku and {{ill|Soga no Umako|en|Soga no Umako|WD=Q1191233|lt=Soga no Umako|1=Soga no Umako}} are said to have collected records from the imperial court and various prominent families. When unclear records of the Age of the Gods were encountered, documents were allegedly discovered in a sacred repository belonging to the ancestral deities of the {{ill|Imibe|ja|忌部|1=Inbe clan|lt=Inbe|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imibe|13=User:Immanuelle/Inbe clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 忌部氏}} and {{ill|Radical 25|en|Radical 25|WD=Q94724|lt=Urabe|1=Radical 25}} clans, upon which Prince Shōtoku based his compilation.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://core.ac.uk/download/pdf/15917108.pdf |title=The "Shindai Kōtai Taiseikyō" Preface |access-date=2024-09-13 |publisher=Tokyo Gakugei University}}</ref>
The text appeared in the late Edo period, during a time when rationality was emphasized following the Confucian-Buddhist debates. It adopts a syncretic perspective, integrating Shinto, Confucianism, and Buddhism.
Despite being declared a forgery by the shogunate, it had influential advocates, including prominent intellectuals and officials of the time. For example, Nagano Uji used it as a reference for {{ill|Mononobe Shinto|ja|物部神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe Shinto|1=Mononobe Shinto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895461}}, and figures like daimyo {{ill|Kuroda Naoaki|ja|黒田直邦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Naoaki|1=Kuroda Naokuni|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678653}}, scholar {{ill|Henmui|ja|偏無為|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Henmui|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and monk {{ill|Tōrei Enji|en|Tōrei Enji|WD=Q17426718|lt=Tōrei Enji|1=Tōrei Enji}} were deeply involved with the text.<ref name=":2" />
== Contents == Many versions of the text exist. The most well-known is the 72-volume edition published in Edo in 1679, with 40 volumes released at the time, including two appendices.
The work is divided into 38 volumes of main content and 34 volumes of auxiliary content, encompassing a wide range of topics, including history, rituals, divination, medical science, prophecy, and constitutional principles, asserting itself as a canonical text of Shinto.
=== Main Content === * '''Chronicle of the Age of the Gods''' (神代本紀): 1 volume. * '''Chronicle of Pre-Heaven''' (先天本紀): 1 volume. * '''Chronicle of Yin and Yang''' (陰陽本紀): 1 volume. * '''Chronicle of Yomi''' (黄泉本紀): 1 volume. * '''Chronicle of Kami''' (神祇本紀): 2 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Rituals''' (神事本紀): 2 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Heavenly Deities''' (天神本紀): 2 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Earthly Deities''' (地祇本紀): 2 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Imperial Descendants''' (皇孫本紀): 2 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Heavenly Descendants''' (天孫本紀): 2 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Divine Sovereigns''' (神皇本紀): 6 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Heavenly Sovereigns''' (天皇本紀): 6 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Imperial Sovereigns''' (帝皇本紀): 6 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Sacred Sovereigns''' (聖皇本紀): 4 volumes.
=== Auxiliary Content === * '''Chronicle of Teachings''' (經教本紀): 6 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Prayers''' (祝言本紀): 1 volume. * '''Chronicle of Heavenly Governance''' (天政本紀): 1 volume. * '''Chronicle of Divination''' (太占本紀): 2 volumes. * '''Chronicle of History''' (歴道本紀): 4 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Medical Practices''' (醫綱本紀): 4 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Rites''' (禮綱本紀): 4 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Songs''' (詠歌本紀): 2 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Teachings''' (御語本紀): 4 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Military Affairs''' (軍旅本紀): 2 volumes. * '''Chronicle of Predictions''' (未然本紀): 1 volume. * '''Chronicle of Constitutional Principles''' (憲法本紀): 1 volume. * '''Chronicle of Shrines''' (神社本紀): 1 volume. * '''Chronicle of Provincial Governors''' (國造本紀): 1 volume.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kujiki|en|Kujiki|WD=Q2716751|lt=Sendai Kuji Hongi|1=Kujiki}} * {{ill|Mononobe Shinto|ja|物部神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe Shinto|1=Mononobe Shinto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895461}}
== References == {{reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/sendaikuji/taisei.htm Sendai Kuji Hongi Taiseikyō] * {{Wayback|url=http://www.ceres.dti.ne.jp/~alex-x/shosai/gisi-001.html |title=World of Forged Histories and Texts |date=1999-08-27}} * [http://www.mars.dti.ne.jp/~techno/column/sendai.htm Sendai Kuji Hongi and Taiseikyō]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|先代旧事本紀大成経|version=102317231|comment=Imported full ja history}}
